posts.title: How to Create a Free QR Code for a Website, Menu, or Event
posts.excerpt: Learn how to create a free QR code for a website, cafe menu, event page, form, or hotel guest guide using DJAI's free browser-based QR generator.
posts.seoTitle: How to Create a Free QR Code for a Website, Menu, or Event
posts.seoDescription: Step-by-step tutorial for creating a free QR code with no account, no watermark, and PNG or SVG download support.
posts.author: DJAI Academy
posts.content:
## Why QR codes are still useful

QR codes are one of the simplest ways to connect offline attention to an online destination. A cafe can place a QR code on a table tent, a hotel can put one inside a guest room, and a course team can use one on a flyer or event banner.

The goal is not to make people type a long URL. The goal is to let them scan once and land in the right place.

For example, a local business like [Siamese Cat Cafe](https://siamesecat.cafe/) could use a QR code for a menu, while [Siamese Cat Hotel](https://hotel.siamesecat.cafe/) could use one for guest information, booking instructions, or local recommendations.

## Tool used in this tutorial

Use the [Free QR Code Generator by DJAI Academy](https://www.djai.academy/tools/qrgen/en/). It creates QR codes directly in the browser and lets you download the result as PNG or SVG.

## Step 1: Open the QR generator

Go to [DJAI's free QR code tool](https://www.djai.academy/tools/qrgen/en/). You do not need to create an account, install software, or upload anything to a server.

The tool opens with a destination URL field and a live preview area. This makes it easy to test the QR code before downloading it.

## Step 2: Paste the destination link

Paste the page you want people to visit. Good QR code destinations include:

- A website homepage
- A menu page
- A booking page
- A Google Form
- A payment page
- A landing page for a course or promotion
- A portfolio or social profile

If you are promoting DJAI training, you can link directly to the [DJAI Academy course page](https://www.djai.academy/course/en/). If you need a custom campaign page or internal tool, the [DJAI development service](https://www.djai.academy/service/en/) can help build one.

## Step 3: Choose the style

After entering the link, adjust the QR pattern, corners, and color. Keep the code easy to scan. Strong contrast works best, especially for printed cards, posters, stickers, and menus.

Avoid making the QR code too small. If it will be printed, test it on the actual material before producing many copies.

## Step 4: Add a simple frame

A short label such as Scan Me or Menu helps users understand what the code does. This is useful in public spaces where people may not know whether the QR code opens a menu, booking form, payment page, or course information.

## Step 5: Download PNG or SVG

Choose PNG for normal web and print use. Choose SVG when you need a scalable file for design software, signs, posters, or professional print work.

If your team makes social posts, flyers, or campaign artwork, the [Siamese Cat Creative Club](https://creative.siamesecat.cafe/) is a useful reference for creative production and visual workflows.

## QR code checklist

- Use a short, stable destination URL
- Test the QR code on two or more phones
- Keep the QR code high contrast
- Do not place it too close to an edge
- Add a clear label or call to action
- Use SVG for large print materials

## Final recommendation

Start with the [DJAI Free QR Code Generator](https://www.djai.academy/tools/qrgen/en/) and create one QR code for one clear action. The best QR codes do not try to do too much. They send people to one useful destination quickly.

posts.title: How to Convert JPG, PNG, and WebP Images for Free
posts.excerpt: A practical guide to converting images between JPG, PNG, and WebP using DJAI Image Tools without uploading your files to a server.
posts.seoTitle: How to Convert JPG, PNG, and WebP Images for Free
posts.seoDescription: Convert JPG, PNG, and WebP images for free in your browser. Learn which format to choose for websites, social content, and business files.
posts.author: DJAI Academy
posts.content:
## Why image format matters

The file format you choose affects image quality, file size, transparency, and website speed. Choosing the right format can make a website feel faster and make a design workflow easier.

Use JPG when you need a small photo file. Use PNG when you need transparency or crisp graphics. Use WebP when you want modern web compression and strong quality at a smaller size.

## Tool used in this tutorial

Use [DJAI Image Tools](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/) to convert JPG, PNG, and WebP images directly in your browser. Your files stay on your device and are not uploaded to DJAI servers.

You can also start from the full [Free DJAI Tools hub](https://www.djai.academy/tools/en/) if you want to see the current public tools.

## Step 1: Open DJAI Image Tools

Go to [Free Image Converter and Resizer](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/). The upload area supports JPG, JPEG, PNG, and WebP.

Click Select Image or drag your file onto the upload area. The tool will show image information such as file type, size, and dimensions.

## Step 2: Pick the output format

Choose the format that matches your goal:

- JPG for photos, small file size, and broad compatibility
- PNG for transparency, screenshots, logos, and sharp graphics
- WebP for faster websites and modern compression

For a cafe website such as [Siamese Cat Cafe](https://siamesecat.cafe/), WebP can help product photos and menu images load faster. For creative assets shared by [Siamese Cat Creative Club](https://creative.siamesecat.cafe/), PNG can be better when transparency matters.

## Step 3: Keep or resize the dimensions

If you only need format conversion, keep the original dimensions. If the image is too large, reduce the width before exporting. A 4000 pixel wide photo is usually unnecessary for a blog thumbnail, website banner, or social preview.

Common website widths include:

- 1200 px for blog images
- 1600 px for large website banners
- 1080 px for square social posts
- 1920 px for full-width hero images

## Step 4: Export and download

After choosing the output format and size settings, process the image and download the result. The browser creates the converted image locally.

This is useful for teams that care about privacy. You do not need to send client images, campaign graphics, or internal screenshots to a third-party conversion server.

## Which format should you use?

### JPG

JPG is best for photos with many colors. It is not good for transparent images, because JPG does not support transparency.

### PNG

PNG is best for transparent graphics, interface screenshots, icons, and text-heavy images. It can be larger than JPG or WebP.

### WebP

WebP is best for modern websites that need smaller files without heavy quality loss. It is a strong option for landing pages, blog images, and product photos.

If your business needs a complete website or automated image workflow, review the [DJAI software development service](https://www.djai.academy/service/en/) or the [Siamese Cat Dev profile](https://www.djai.academy/siamese_cat/dev/en/).

## Final recommendation

Use [DJAI Image Tools](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/) when you need a fast, private image conversion workflow. Start with WebP for websites, PNG for transparency, and JPG for simple photo sharing.

posts.title: How to Compress an Image to 100 KB or 500 KB Without Uploading It
posts.excerpt: Learn how to reduce an image to a target file size such as 100 KB, 200 KB, or 500 KB using a private browser-based image compression tool.
posts.seoTitle: How to Compress an Image to 100 KB or 500 KB Without Uploading It
posts.seoDescription: Step-by-step guide to compressing images to a target KB size directly in your browser with DJAI Image Tools.
posts.author: DJAI Academy
posts.content:
## Why target file size compression is useful

Many websites, application forms, booking systems, and content platforms require images below a certain file size. You may need a profile photo below 100 KB, a document image below 200 KB, or a website image below 500 KB.

The problem is that normal image editors often ask you to guess the right quality setting. A target size tool is easier because you can tell it the result you want.

## Tool used in this tutorial

Use [DJAI Image Tools](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/) and choose the target file size workflow. The tool processes the image in your browser, so your image is not uploaded to a server.

This privacy-first approach is important for businesses, schools, hospitality teams, and creators who handle client images or operational documents.

## Step 1: Choose your image

Open [DJAI Image Tools](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/) and select the JPG, PNG, or WebP image you want to compress.

If the original image is very large, the tool may need to reduce both quality and dimensions to reach a small target size. This is normal. A 5000 pixel image cannot always become 100 KB while staying visually perfect.

## Step 2: Select a target size

Choose a target such as:

- 50 KB for small forms or thumbnails
- 100 KB for profile photos and application forms
- 200 KB for document uploads
- 500 KB for website images and blog graphics
- 1 MB for larger high-quality uploads

If you are preparing a guide, promotion, or business page for [DJAI Academy](https://www.djai.academy/en/), 500 KB is usually a reasonable target for a large web image. For a lightweight directory page or a mobile-first landing page, 100 KB to 200 KB may be better.

## Step 3: Keep dimensions only when needed

Some images need exact dimensions. Others only need a smaller file size. If the file size target is strict, allow the tool to reduce dimensions when necessary.

For example, a hotel guest guide for [Siamese Cat Hotel](https://hotel.siamesecat.cafe/) might use smaller web images so the page loads quickly for travelers on mobile data. A creative portfolio image from [Siamese Cat Creative Club](https://creative.siamesecat.cafe/) might use a higher target size to preserve visual detail.

## Step 4: Compare the original and result

After processing, compare:

- Original file size
- New file size
- Original dimensions
- New dimensions
- Percentage saved

If the result looks too compressed, choose a larger target size or export as WebP. If the result is still too large, reduce the width or height.

## Step 5: Download the compressed image

Once the image is ready, download the result and use it in your website, form, campaign, article, or internal workflow.

For teams that repeatedly prepare images, a custom tool can save hours. DJAI builds business tools, automation systems, and internal platforms through [Develop With DJAI](https://www.djai.academy/service/en/).

## Best practices for smaller images

- Resize dimensions before compressing heavily
- Use WebP for websites when possible
- Keep PNG only when transparency matters
- Avoid uploading giant camera photos directly to web pages
- Test the image on mobile screens
- Keep important text readable after compression

## Final recommendation

Use [DJAI Image Tools](https://www.djai.academy/tools/resizeimg/en/) whenever you need to compress an image to 100 KB, 200 KB, 500 KB, or another target size. It is fast, private, free, and practical for everyday business use.
